video.title: Puncheurs Battle It Out On 26% Mur De Huy | La Flèche Wallonne 2022 Men's Highlights

all_candidates.title: Puncheurs Battle It Out On 26% Mur De Huy | La Flèche Wallonne 2022 Men's Highlights
Favourites Rip It Up Over Brutal Ardennes Climbs | La Flèche Wallonne 2022 Women's Highlights

transcript.clean_text: Welcome to short highlights of the 86th edition of La Flèche Wallonne. Don't forget you can catch this race back in its entirety and all the highlights form on GCN+ and Discovery Plus. Let's look at the course for this edition, which is the 40th time this race has finished in Huy on the Mur de Huy, the wall of Huy, alongside the Meuse River. They do two and a half laps of our finishing circuit to complete 202 km in its entirety for this midweek Ardennes classic. This was our breakaway of 10 riders. It was eight originally and then joined by 10. A lot of hard driving being done by Daryl Impey and Bruno Armirail in there, too. Nice combination of riders, mix of too. And the peloton happy to let them sit there and gain around about 3 minutes. Ineos Grenadiers and uh, Groupama-FDJ combining well and Jens Keukeleire was one of the first of those to be absorbed back into our peloton once detached. Once we're onto our finishing circuit, the combination of the Côte de Cherave and the Côte de Ahffe were the uh, what eventually put paid to our breakaway. Tom Pidcock there being dropped from the peloton, still not quite over the issues he's had in the last few weeks, as well. This was the attack by Jan Bakelants of Intermarché-Wanty-Gobert, big attack, which was then followed shortly after was by this incredible drive from Alberto Bettiol setting up the British rider Simon Carr of EF Education EasyPost. Great ride by uh, the British rider who's grown up in France, riding in the pink of EF Education EasyPost. But as we got back into the second of those laps on our finishing circuit, the familiar figure of Geraint Thomas of Ineos Grenadiers leading the uh, the main peloton chasing things down with Tiesj Benoot just alongside him waiting. There were only four riders left with one lap to go. And on that final lap, gradually seconds being drilled down as Simon Carr joined the leading group in the pink there. But this is a leading group that was starting to run out of firepower despite their efforts. Simon Carr kicking on by himself. Jimmy Janssens, the Belgian and Alpecin-Fenix trying to go with him. But that breakaway not quite having the legs. In the end, it was just two of them left. Valentin Ferron was out front with Simon Carr. Ferron doing a great ride to survive from that breakaway, but it was into that final lap. A lot of hard work being done particularly by Geraint Thomas, the former Tour de France champion for Ineos Grenadiers to bring things back together. And with 9K to go, they were fully back together. An opportunity for the big teams to set things up for the likes of Julian Alaphilippe, Alejandro Valverde, both of whom are multiple winners of this race. Valverde five times, of course, and Julian Alaphilippe looking to try and get his fourth victory in La Flèche Wallonne. In between the two main climbs this time, a big attack from Cofidis and Rémy Rochas, the Frenchman, trying to stay clear. In the end, though, one rider going right over the top of him, Søren Kragh Andersen with a big attack. And Mauri Van Sevenant, the last of those riders who could latch on with Søren Kragh Andersen. They would be the final two who were out front as we came to the base of the wall, the Mur de Huy for the final time. Søren Kragh Andersen led onto it, but the inevitable was about to happen. Enric Mas, the Spaniard from Movistar, did everything he could for his team leader and five-time victor Alejandro Valverde to try and get Valverde into position. But the brutal brutal 18-19% hairpins of the Mur de Huy set up Dylan Teuns, the Belgian for Bahrain Victorious, to try and challenge Valverde. He challenged him, and Valverde laid down arms to take a brilliant second place. The Belgian Dylan Teuns became the first victor since Philippe Gilbert for his home nation of Belgium on home soil and what a popular winner in the colors of Bahrain Victorious. Dylan Teuns, not even time to zip up the jersey, but plenty of time to celebrate. The Russian Alexandr Vlasov getting a great third place after a fantastic final climb. The defending champion Julian Alaphilippe coming in fourth just ahead of Dani Martínez, Michael Woods. Dylan Teuns though tops the podium just ahead of Valverde and Vlasov. Do hope you've enjoyed it. My name is Jez Cox. Do join us here at the home of cycling for coverage of Liège-Bastogne-Liège this coming Sunday. Bye-bye.
